desc: Lower Rota Vista Park is a hidden gem known to serious birders for its unique colony of Pelagic Cormorants that nest beneath the Warren Avenue Bridge. The park is a prime spot for watching nesting peregrine falcons. To visit the park, go to Elizabeth Avenue's dead end. It's as simple as that. Take the stairs down to the park's 130-foot-long shoreline. The park has a picnic table and a few benches.
accessibility: Parking: Limited street parking on Elizabeth Ave | Wheelchair Access: No | Restrooms: None | Terrain: Staircase down to grassy viewpoint | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: This 20 acre family, pet and recreation friendly park has 1,900 feet of public accessible shoreline, children's play area that is ADA accessible, group shelter, picnic tables, BBQs, 2 half-court basketball, boat launch, sand volleyball court, public restrooms and 75 parking spaces. Home to only 911 Memorial in Kitsap region, and Bremerton World War I Memorial. The park has great views of Smith Cove and the Port Washington Narrows
accessibility: Parking: Large paved lot with ADA stalls| Wheelchair Access: Yes | Restrooms: None | Terrain: Paved trails around waterfront | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: Erlands Point Preserve is a 30-acre county park located just downstream of Chico Salmon Park, which is typically the primary salmon viewing site for Salmon Tours.   Erlands Point Preserve contains the main stem of Chico Creek, several side channels that serve as important salmon rearing habitat, and a large man-made pond that are all hydrologically connected.  It also contains a large forested area with walking trails.
accessibility: Parking: Pull-out off Earlands Point Road NW | Wheelchair Access: No | Restrooms: None | Terrain: 0.8 mi path | Dogs Allowed: No

desc: Kitsap County Heritage Park is 570 acres of preserved lands, and has about five miles of trails in the area of the preserve that is open to the public. Illahee Creek is a salmon-bearing stream, and there are myriad types of wildlife in this area to enjoy. Please respect any signs restricting public access — as a preserve, part of the mission is to provide protected areas for animals to thrive away from human interaction.
accessibility: Parking: Large paved lot with ADA stalls| Wheelchair Access: No | Restrooms: None | Terrain: Mostly wide flat trails | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: Find this tiny gem of a state park on the Kitsap Peninsula, tucked between Bainbridge Island and Bremerton. Vistors here will find prime beachside meandering and a relaxing atmosphere, enhanced by the dense forest typical of land in the Puget Sound area. If you want to extend your day, or simply want more hiking, head to nearby Illahee Forest Preserve for a little more rugged experience.
accessibility: Parking: Large paved lots with ADA stall. Lot requires park fee or Discovery pass| Wheelchair Access: Limited on trails | Restrooms: Multiple restrooms throughout park | Terrain: Around 0.5 miles of trails including a steep staircase down to lake.| Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: This park sits at the northern tip of Dyes Inlet and just outside of the urban center of Silverdale. From the parking area a network of trails extends out through the forest. There are two distinct loops that form a figure 8 as well as a couple side trails that go along the shore. Visitors to this park can stay within the park boundary and explore the trails here, or use this as a jumping off point for the Clear Creek Trail which carries on north as well as west. This park is just one link in this longer trail system. Habitats include pebble beach and open bay; marine, estuarine and riparian habitats. Purple martin nest box site.
accessibility: Parking: Limited spots in paved lot including one ADA stall| Wheelchair Access: Limited on trails | Restrooms: Multiple restrooms throughout park | Terrain: Wide flat gravel path| Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: Aquatic and wooded habitats, open fields. This park hosts Hooded Merganser and Pied-billed Grebe, and warblers in migration. Buck Lake County park is part of an extensive trail system called the Hansville Greenway.
accessibility: Parking: Paved lot | Wheelchair Access: No | Restrooms: Park restrooms open seasonally | Terrain: Extensive trail network of varying surface types and conditions | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: A designated IBA (Important Bird Area) â€˘ Extensive views of Admiralty Inlet â€˘ Marine, sandy beach, marsh, and wooded habitats â€˘ Best known for alcids, loons and cormorants, as well as large numbers of Bonaparte's and Heermann's gulls in the fall â€˘ Viewing platform, trails and beach access â€˘ Portable toilet, limited handicap access.
accessibility: Parking: Large paved lot with handicap accesible spots | Wheelchair Access: Limited | Restrooms: ADA Restrooms | Terrain: Paved path to lighthouse, varying trail types and width through wetland and forest section | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: Small, clean beach and park featuring picnic tables, sea-glass foraging and views of Kingston harbor. Marine and slough habitats. Opposite side of Appletree Cove with added view into Carpenter Creek slough.
accessibility: Parking: Small parking lot| Wheelchair Access: Limited, views of water from lot | Restrooms:Portable toilet | Terrain: Flat grassy areas and undeveloped beach | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: Norwegian Point Park is one of three Kitsap County Parks in Hansville and provides access to Puget Sound and wonderful views towards Whidbey Island on the site of an old fishing resort.
accessibility: Parking: Paved lot off Twin Spits Road | Wheelchair Access: Limtited | Restrooms: Portable restroom | Terrain: Gazebo with ramp, undeveloped saltwater access | Dogs Allowed: No

desc: Marine habitat of the Hood Canal, wooded slopes, open lawns, bluff viewing platform, small park, no facilities
link: Port Gamble is one of Washingtonďż˝s most beautifully preserved 19th-century towns, a National Historic Landmark overlooking the sparkling waters of Hood Canal. Visitors can stroll maple- and elm-lined streets, admire New Englandďż˝style architecture, browse unique shops, and explore the townďż˝s rich milling historyďż˝all in a compact, walkable waterfront village. Beyond its historic beauty, Port Gamble offers access to miles of nearby forest trails, scenic viewpoints, kayaking spots, and peaceful beaches, making it an ideal hub for outdoor adventure across North Kitsap.
accessibility: Parking: Street parking available in Port Gamble | Wheelchair Access: Limtited | Restrooms: Restrooms can be found in businesses around Port Gamble | Terrain: Varying terrain around the bay including mowed grass and concrete building footprints | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: This small park is located on the shoreline next to the Hood Canal Bridge. A popular spot for boaters, this park also has something to offer hikers. A short path leaves from the beach and wanders through the forest along Whitford Road. Along the way a couple benches offer places to sit and rest, and the beach also makes for a nice place to hang out.
accessibility: Parking: Large paved lot | Wheelchair Access: Limtited, access to waterfront and seasonal pier from parking lot | Restrooms: Seasonal restrooms | Terrain: Flat terrain and path | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

sitename: Poulsbo Waterfront to Oyster Plant Park

desc: Meandering along its namesake creek in Kitsap County, from Dyes Inlet in the south to Schold Farm in the north, Clear Creek Trail offers visitors a unique blend of urban accessibility and natural wonder. This family-friendly trail system winds its way through a diverse landscape of riparian areas, wetlands, and riparian forests. Built with accessibility in mind, most of the trail consists of hard-packed gravel surfaces and thoughtfully constructed boardwalks spanning wetlands and bridges crossing the creek. The Clear Creek Trail is a testament to community cooperation, built on both public and private land to preserve natural spaces within our urban environment.  Clear Creek Trail offers something for everyone, whether you're a casual walker, an eager bird watcher, or a cycling enthusiast. Come explore its winding paths and discover the natural beauty of Kitsap County.
accessibility: Parking: Paved lot at Silverdale Rotary Gateway Park | Wheelchair Access: Yes | Restrooms:  Pit toilet at Silverdale Dog Park | Terrain: 2.1 miles are hikeable via wheelchair starting from Silverdale Rotary Gateway Park with 73 foot elevation gain and minimal cross-slope. The trail consists of boardwalk, packed dirt and paved sections. About 50 feet of the trail has 2-3 inch stones jutting up from surface making for a very rocky ride on freeway side of wetlands area.  | Dogs Allowed: Yes

desc: This quick hike leads to a lovely wide sandy beach at the mouth of a marshy wetland. The trail winds through Douglas-fir, cedar, hemlock and alder along a broad wetland meadow. Be on the lookout for shorebirds and waterfowl as you hike. At the beach you'll find a profusion of russet hued Pacific madrone adorning a sheer bluff. Look for shells and crabs along the shoreline and enjoy a broad view of the Olympic Mountains to the west.
accessibility: Parking: Limited parking on shoulder of Twin Spits Rd | Wheelchair Access: None | Restrooms:  Portable Restroom | Terrain: Flat dirt path through forest. 1 Mile roundtrip to beach. | Dogs Allowed: no

desc: Shine Tidelands State Park is a day-use park with a sandy shoreline along Bywater Bay. A peaceful stop while traveling through the Kitsap Peninsula or for admiring stunning views of Hood Canal, this park offers a picnic area and beachy fun.
accessibility: Parking: Parking lot requires fee or Discovery Pass | Wheelchair Access: None | Restrooms:  None | Terrain: Gravel lot with access to undeveloped beach and shoreline | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

sitename: Klingel Bryan Bear Wetland Wildlife Preserve

desc: Hike, walk the dog, hunt for mushrooms, or grab your mountain bike and go! With 635 acres to roam and 29 miles of trail you are likely to have the forest to yourself. Be sure to wear your waterproof hiking boots as the trails can get a bit muddy. It is easy to lose yourself in the solitude of the dense second-growth forest as 29 miles of rustic trails meander through 635 acres, offering an occasional glimpse of a black-tailed deer or Douglas squirrel. If you are lucky you can also spot black bears, cougars, coyotes, raccoons, and beavers, as well as the many species of birds that call Banner Forest home.
accessibility: Parking: Gravel Lot | Wheelchair Access: No | Restrooms: Portable Restroom | Terrain: Flat dirt path with varying widths and conditions | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: This section of the larger Hansville Greenway has 1 mile of accessible trails including a viewing platform over the Alder Wetland.
accessibility: Parking: No dedicated parking lot. Trailheads can be accessed from NE Twin Spits Rd or Shnoreview Dr NE | Wheelchair Access: Yes | Restrooms: No | Terrain: 1 mile of wheelchair accessible trail | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: Guillemot Cove Nature Reserve is one of the hidden gems of Kitsap County Parks, with stunning scenic vistas of the Olympic Mountains, beautiful hiking trails through mature forests, and a rustic old house carved out of a cedar stump. It has a large parking area along Stavis Bay Road south of Seabeck, and offers several trails. Visitors to Guillemot Cove Nature Reserve start their adventure by hiking through groves of mature bigleaf maples and tunnels of huckleberry down to a wetland where Boycee Creek meets the Hood Canal. Beyond the marsh you will find a secluded beach with some of the best views of the Olympics, and especially The Brothers. This diverse mix of habitats (mature forests, riparian, wetland, and shoreline) are home to over 70 species of birds, including eagles, a variety of warblers, and seabirds like pigeon guillemots, loons, and black scoters. Visitors to Guillemot Cove Nature Reserve can delight in the natural soundscape of birdsong, rustling leaves, and lapping waves.
accessibility: Parking: Large parking area along Stavis Bay Road south of Seabeck | Wheelchair Access: None | Restrooms:  None | Terrain: 2.3 mile roundtrip to Dickerson Falls with trail varying in width and condition | Dogs Allowed: No

desc: Port Gamble Forest Heritage Park protects one of the largest lowland forests remaining in west Puget Sound. This vast forested landscape provides important habitat for bears, salmon, birds, and other wildlife, protects the waters of beautiful Port Gamble Bay, and contains cherished open space cherished as a place for year-round outdoor recreation.  The trail options are endless along the old service roads and recreational trails of this 3,400-acre park a favorite of mountain bikers, trail runners, equestrians, and hikers. If it is water views you want, enjoy a quiet walk along the bluff trail overlooking Port Gamble Bay.
accessibility: Parking: ADA Lot with 75 parking stalls at North entrance | Wheelchair Access: limited | Restrooms:  None | Terrain: 2.3 mile roundtrip to Dickerson Falls with trail varying in width and condition | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: The 2,200 acre tree farm has become a popular destination for the public as a scenic spot to walk dogs, hike, run, mountain bike or go horseback riding. The Lebers Lane trailhead and parking lot are used by nearly 100,000 visitors a year. Many people walk the short two mile round-trip trail to Dickerson Falls; others challenge themselves by continuing to Zachďż˝s Lookout, hiking the South Loop Trail, or creating their own route among the many forest roads. It is our hope that our small network of private trails and forest roads becomes an integral part of a more extensive Central Kitsap trail network. We envision a trail system that allows people to walk or ride from Otto Jarstad Park through Ueland Tree Farm all the way to Green Mountain State Forest or Anderson Landing Preserve on Hood Canal. We ask all visitors to be alert and responsible when visiting our working tree farm, one that provides rock, gravel, and timber-related products to the community. This includes obeying all signs, staying out of active mine sites and logging areas, and being extra careful around heavy equipment. If you see something suspicious on the property, please report it.
accessibility: Parking: Gravel lot at Leber | Wheelchair Access: None | Restrooms:  None | Terrain: 2.3 mile roundtrip to Dickerson Falls with trail varying in width and condition | Dogs Allowed: Yes on leash
